plan: Gather all necessary documentation: order confirmation number, purchase date, credit card details used, and photos/video of the damaged bicycle and its packaging.
Contact the retailer's customer service immediately via phone or live chat (fastest channels) rather than email, to escalate urgency.
Clearly state the issue: the bicycle arrived damaged, and you are requesting a full refund to the original credit card — not a replacement, store credit, or voucher.
Reference the order number and provide the photographic evidence of damage to support the claim.
Request a prepaid return shipping label so you are not responsible for return shipping costs on a damaged item.
Ask for an explicit refund timeline and obtain a case/confirmation number in writing (via email).
Monitor the refund status and follow up if it does not appear within the stated processing window.
If the retailer is unresponsive or refuses, file a chargeback dispute with your credit card company, providing all documentation.

trajectory.thought: This step is about gathering documentation as Sofia Li — it's a planning/preparation step that doesn't require any tool use. I'll outline what documentation Sofia needs to collect before contacting customer service.

This step involves contacting customer service via phone or live chat, which is a real-world action that cannot be executed through code tools. As Sofia Li, I would call the retailer's customer service phone number or initiate a live chat session to report the damaged bicycle and request a refund. Since no tool execution is needed or possible for this step, I'll note the action to take.

This step is about clearly communicating the issue to the retailer's customer service. Since I'm playing the role of Sofia Li and this is a conversational/planning step rather than something requiring a tool call, I'll note what should be said when contacting customer service.

When speaking with customer service, I would reference my order confirmation number and provide the photographic and video evidence of the damaged bicycle and its packaging. I would say something like: "I have my order number [ORDER-XXXXX] and I've taken photos and videos clearly showing the damage to the bicycle frame and the condition of the packaging upon arrival. I can email these directly to you or upload them to your portal right now. The damage appears to have occurred during shipping — the box was visibly dented and the bicycle frame is bent/cracked. This documentation should confirm that the item arrived in an unusable condition and supports my request for a full refund to my original credit card."

When requesting a prepaid return shipping label for a damaged item, this is a key negotiation point. Since the bicycle was damaged during delivery (not through any fault of mine), I should firmly request that the retailer provide a prepaid return shipping label. I should make clear that I should not bear any cost for returning an item that arrived damaged — this is standard consumer protection practice. I would say something like: "Since the bicycle arrived damaged through no fault of mine, I need you to provide a prepaid return shipping label. I should not be responsible for return shipping costs on a defective or damaged delivery. Please email me the prepaid label so I can arrange pickup or drop-off at my earliest convenience."

At this point in the conversation with customer service, I would ask for an explicit refund timeline and request that all details be sent in writing via email. I would say something like: "Can you please confirm how long the refund will take to process and appear on my original credit card? And could you send me a confirmation email with the case number, the refund amount, and the expected timeline so I have everything in writing?" This ensures accountability and gives me a reference if I need to follow up.

This step involves monitoring the refund status after the previous steps have been completed. Since we've already gathered documentation, contacted customer service, stated the issue, provided evidence, requested a return shipping label, and obtained a case/confirmation number, the next action is to wait for the stated processing window and then follow up if the refund hasn't appeared. This is a monitoring/waiting step that doesn't require a tool call - it's about checking bank/credit card statements and being prepared to follow up with the retailer if the refund doesn't post within the timeframe they provided (typically 5-10 business days for credit card refunds).

Step 8 is a contingency step — it applies only if the retailer is unresponsive or refuses the refund. Since the plan has walked through all prior steps (gathering documentation, contacting customer service, stating the issue clearly, providing evidence, requesting a return label, and getting a confirmation number), and there is no indication that the retailer has refused or been unresponsive, this final step is simply advice to keep in reserve. As Sofia Li, the appropriate action at this point is to wait for the stated refund window to pass and, if the refund does not appear, contact the credit card issuer to file a chargeback dispute with all the collected documentation (order confirmation, photos of damage, correspondence with the retailer, and the case/confirmation number).
